synthetic:1325f17516c8abe6eb3226ac6bdd0c.reason: The regex /<[^>]+>/g on line 164 is indeed bypassable (e.g., <<script>script> survives as <script>), but the resulting description155 is only consumed via cheerio's .attr('content', description155) on lines 169 and 188. Cheerio's HTML serializer ($.root().html() on line 211) properly escapes attribute values, so any residual <script> text is rendered as <script> in the final HTML — inert in attribute context. No HTML element injection is possible.

synthetic:23bc8e664be1083752cbfe9db34571.reason: The flagged file extra/healthcheck.js is explicitly deprecated (see line 6: 'Changed to healthcheck.go, it will be deleted in the future'). The production Docker HEALTHCHECK uses the compiled Go binary extra/healthcheck (docker/dockerfile:40, docker/dockerfile:89), built from extra/healthcheck.go via build-healthcheck.js — not the deprecated JS script. The JS file is never executed in any build or runtime path; it is dead code retained only due to a historical Portainer issue (lines 2-3). The NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0 setting is therefore unreachable in production.

synthetic:97f49a2ebe9d11bb0379cfa1404f8e.reason: The flagged POST /setup-database route (lines 170-293) is part of a one-time bootstrap server that only runs when no database is configured (server/server.js:235-238). It is ephemeral: on successful setup, the server permanently shuts itself down (lines 285-292, calling tempServer.close() and resolve()), after which the main application takes over and this route no longer exists. The runningSetup flag (lines 173-178) enforces sequential mutual exclusion—only one setup attempt executes at a time. The database access is a connectivity smoke test (SELECT 1 at line 264), not a data operation that could leak or corrupt application data. Because the setup server is transient by design and self-terminates after one successful invocation, the absence of rate limiting does not create a practically exploitable condition in this codebase.

synthetic:d380df956d20339294265b20974daa.reason: The username is not sensitive data in this context: it is transmitted in cleartext in the NTLM Type 3 message (ntlm.js:208-212, buf.write(username,...)). The HMAC-MD5 construction (hash.js:60-62) is mandated by the NTLMv2 protocol specification; the HMAC key is the NTLM password hash, not the username. HMAC-MD5 does not rely on MD5's broken collision-resistance properties and remains secure for this protocol-defined use. The data flow is username → toUpperCase() → HMAC-MD5 update, but the username is non-secret and the algorithm choice is protocol-required, not a developer error.

synthetic:e436763b76d8cff5c9861d5c60f1f3.reason: The value stored in user.password is always a bcrypt hash produced by passwordHash.generate() (server/password-hash.js:10-12), never a plaintext password. At server/model/user.js:45, shake256() hashes this already-bcrypt-hashed value for JWT invalidation checks (server/server.js:415), not for password storage or verification. Since the input to shake256 is already a strong bcrypt hash, the use of a fast SHAKE256 hash does not constitute insufficient password hashing — no plaintext password reaches the flagged sink.
